It evaluates evidence-based strategies that can be implemented to improve the CC ‘screen-triage-treat’approach, including HPV DNA self-sampling.Objectives:1) Review the literature on existing mHealth interventions used to address women’s cancers in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). 2) Evaluate the effectiveness of evidencebased recruitment strategies in increasing attendance to visual inspection of the cervix with/through acetic acid (VIA). 3) Understand CC risk and willingness to attend screening at the participant level. 4) Assess the effectiveness of mHealth-supported patient navigation (PN) strategies to increase adherence to VIA triage and treatment of precancerous lesions.Methods:1) Conduct a scoping review through a search strategy of five electronic databases and compare outcomes against the mHealth evidence reporting and assessment (WHO-mERA) checklist. 2) Eligible women were recruited from 30 communities (wards) in Tanzania to perform HPV DNA self-sampling to compare evidence-based recruitment strategies. 3) Participants completed exit surveys after undergoing HPV DNA self-sampling. 4) HPV-positive participants are being randomized to one of four PN conditions in an ongoing randomized controlled trial.Results:1) 30 studies met the inclusion criteria. More than 50% of the studies provided enough information to assess a minimum of 10 items on the WHO-mERA checklist. 2) The preliminary  analysis revealed no significant difference in attendance to VIA (if HPV-positive) between the recruitment arms. 3) Despite high awareness of CC among participants, significant knowledge gaps and misconceptions remain regarding CC and HPV risk. However, when provided with CC counselling and screening, most participants felt well-informed and trusted and relied on their partners’ input in making health-related decisions. 4) Delivering evidence-based strategies to improve linkage to preventive care for CC is ongoing as a culmination of the completed work to date in the research program.Conclusions:Implementing the selected innovative, evidence-based strategies in Tanzania’s cervical cancer prevention ‘screen-triage-treat’pathway will inform health system change in the national CC screening program and other countries with a similar cancer-health disparity.

title: Characterizing the Experience of Black Americans Seeking Mental Health Services in Rural Oklahoma
abstract: BACKGROUND: Rural Black Americans face mental health disparities due to the intersection of race, geography, and systemic barriers. Mental health services are limited, and utilization remains low, highlighting the need for comprehensive research on barriers and facilitators impacting service use.OBJECTIVES: This dissertation explored the mental health service landscape for rural Black communities by (1) conducting a scoping review of available literature, (2) investigating provider perspectives on service delivery challenges, and (3) examining community members' experiences with mental health service utilization.METHODS: A scoping review identified studies on rural Black Americans and mental health services. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with mental health providers and community members. Thematic analysis identified barriers, facilitators, needs, and challenges in service delivery and utilization. Findings from each study were triangulated.RESULTS: The review highlighted community-oriented and electronic services as key resources. Providers reported mistrust, regulatory and legislative constraints, and inadequate mental health literacy as barriers, and community engagement and diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) initiatives in care delivery as service use facilitators. Community members reported cultural skepticism, stigma, and geographic isolation as major barriers, and faith and social support systems as facilitators.DISCUSSION: Triangulation of the studies identified interconnected barriers to mental health access for rural Black communities including economic strain, low mental health literacy, logistical challenges, geographic isolation, and cultural mistrust. Shared facilitators included community trust, faith-based support, and culturally relevant care. This dissertation advocates for an integrated approach involving economic support, culturally relevant education, policy reform, and partnerships with community and faith-based organizations to increase mental health service utilization among rural Black communities.CONCLUSION: Barriers to mental health service utilization are rooted in social and cultural contexts, but community-based and faith-driven programs offer pathways for improving access. Providers and community members emphasized the need for culturally tailored interventions, collaborative policy development, and ongoing support for providers serving rural Black communities. Future research should focus on co-developing solutions with these communities to address mental health disparities.

title: COVID-19, Black Adults, and Mental Health Interventions: A Scoping Review
abstract: Black people are disproportionately affected by COVID-19, and are more likely to have mental health illnesses as a result of COVID-19, when compared to White people. To synthesize knowledge of the mental health interventions available to Black people in the context of COVID-19, using a scoping review approach, 10 databases were searched for relevant sources of evidence. The scoping review included 17 studies that reported knowledge generation about mental health or implemented an intervention to address mental health issues in the context of COVID-19. Of these, five implemented an intervention to address the mental health issues associated with the COVID-19 pandemic. One study utilized unidirectional text messaging to provide information to the participants, and another study utilized phone calls. The other three studies that implemented an intervention utilized guided meditation, nutrition education, and wellness pod meetings. These studies compared mental health indicators before and after intervention to measure the impact of the intervention. All five studies that implemented an intervention recorded improvements in some or all of the mental health indicators measured. Seven studies collected quantitative data about mental health using questionnaires, and two collected qualitative data through interviews and focused groups. Findings revealed a paucity in the number of interventions that have been conducted to address mental health issues in the context of COVID-19 that are available to Black adults. Future research should explore how to expand and replicate the impactful interventions, and stakeholders should advocate for additional guidelines to aid the implementation of culturally appropriate mental health interventions.

title: Closing the Know-Do Gap in Global Health in Sub-Saharan Africa: a Critical Scoping Review of Knowledge Translation Practices in Global Health Research Partnerships
abstract: Introduction: Sub-Saharan Africa is unlikely to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals by 2030. With poor health outcomes and a dependence on external funding, global health research partnerships (GHRPs) with high-income countries are criticized for being inequitable and inadequately representing local researchers.Objective: This study examined the barriers and facilitators to Knowledge Translation (KT) in GHRPs in Sub-Saharan Africa, critiquing them through the lens of local researchers’ experiences and the degree of congruence with partnership principles in contemporary literature.Methods: A critical scoping review of literature from five databases was conducted to identify barriers and facilitators to KT. Alignment with published GHRP principles was assessed using a rubric.Conclusion: Evaluating GHRPs and identifying KT barriers and facilitators to KT can guide researchers and policymakers in building equitable and efficient partnerships. This process promotes decolonization and co-creating sustainable bridges between research and global health policy and practice in Sub-Saharan Africa

title: Traditional Languages, Cultural Practices, and the Oral Health of First Nations Children: A Strength-Based Approach
abstract: First Nations children in Canada continue to experience oral health and nutritional inequities. The objective of this cross-sectional study was to determine the statistical relationship between traditional languages, cultural practices, and untreated dental caries and malnutrition risk in First Nations children in Northern Ontario and Manitoba. Nested in the Nishtam Niwiipitan (My First Teeth) Study, predictor variables measured speaking traditional languages and cultural practices of caregivers. Outcome variables measured untreated dental caries and malnutrition risk in children. A child’s odds (adjusted for select covariates) of having ≥3 untreated decayed teeth are 59% less (OR 0.41, 95% CI: 0.2 - 0.9, p=.02) if the caregiver speaks a First Nations language versus English daily. A child’s odds of having high malnutrition risk are 40% less if the caregiver speaks a First Nations language versus English daily (OR 0.6, 95% CI: 0.3 - 1.3), however this relationship was not statistically significant. Traditional languages may be harnessed as Indigenous-led, strength-based approaches to oral health programming with First Nations children.

title: Co-Design and Evaluation of a Peer-Led Social Media-Based Food Literacy Intervention for Adolescents From Low-Socioeconomic Status Communities
abstract: Current food literacy interventions may be adapted to improve access for adolescents using peer approaches and social media. Social media is a significant source of health information for adolescents and is ideally suited for peer education, an effective method of health promotion for this population. This thesis sought to examine how a peer-led social media-based food literacy intervention could be co-designed with adolescents from low-socioeconomic status communities and evaluated for feasibility. Paper 1 examined the literature on social media-based nutrition interventions for adolescents to examine feasibility, intervention design, and factors influencing interventions. We found few studies examined feasibility outcomes of interventions and identified a need for interventions to strengthen peer support components. Paper 2 systematically categorized and characterized publicly available food and nutrition content on Instagram according to food literacy conceptual frameworks. Our analysis identified areas for improving food literacy education on low-cost foods and cooking non-Western foods, as well as content on cooking together and sharing recipes, signifying the importance of the social aspect of cooking to social media users. Paper 3 qualitatively determined adolescents’ informational needs and requirements for a peer-led social media-based food literacy program using semi-structured in-depth interviews. We identified that adolescents preferred a community focused program to facilitate their food literacy in a culturally sensitive way and address the importance of families in their food literacy. Paper 4 was the feasibility testing of the co-designed, peer-led, social media-based food literacy program. We identified high acceptability of the intervention among participants and low acceptability with the research study due to technical barriers and perceptions of research. This thesis identified the importance of the socially constructed nature of food literacy and cooking skills and the significance of local context (e.g., community and culture) in peer-led social media-based interventions. Focusing future efforts on these aspects of food literacy interventions can ensure adolescents access effectively designed food literacy education and develop confidence in their food skills prior to adulthood.

title: Serum Soluble Epoxide Hydrolase Derived Oxylipins, Cognition and Depression in Individuals With Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
abstract: Individuals with type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) are at increased risk of developing cognitive decline and depressive symptoms, and inflammation has been hypothesized to be a common factor. This thesis first summarizes blood markers of inflammation (i.e., cytokines and other proteins) commonly associated with cognitive decline in T2DM in the literature, followed by an exploration of the cytochrome P450-soluble epoxide hydrolase (CYP450-sEH) pathway in cognition and depression among those with T2DM. The CYP450-sEH pathway produces pro-resolving and inflammatory lipid markers and has been implicated in diabetes-associated cognitive impairment and depression in animal models; however previous studies have neglected to study such markers in favour of cytokines and other proteins.The over-arching hypothesis is that while current blood markers of inflammation will be implicated in cognitively impaired T2DM, there will be considerable heterogeneity in these relationships, suggesting the need to explore alternative pathways to identify, quantify and understand inflammation in this context. First, a comprehensive meta-analysis integrating data from 40 studies found an association between cognitive impairment in T2DM and increased levels of blood interleukin-6 (IL-6) and C-reactive protein (CRP), alongside decreased concentrations of brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF). These findings support an inflammatory-vascular interaction contributing to cognitive decline in T2DM; however, meta-analyses for these markers reported considerable heterogeneity, suggesting the need to examine alternative mechanisms.Next, the CYP450-sEH pathway was studied in relation to cognition in individuals with T2DM. Several lipid metabolites were linked to lower executive function and verbal memory scores, with interactions suggesting obesity-dependent relationships. Depression also was studied as it commonly presents in T2DM and can predispose individuals to cognitive decline and dementia. The CYP450-sEH pathway was implicated in depressed individuals with T2DM, and depressive severity correlated with altered levels of metabolites, suggesting a dysregulation in pro-resolving lipid mediators which may contribute to the pathogenesis of depression. These studies may represent the first steps in understanding the potential role of the CYP450-sEH pathway in clinically relevant cognitive and depressive symptoms in older adults with T2DM, which could offer therapeutic avenues to mitigate the burden of comorbidities in this population.

title: The Effect of High and Low Glycemic Index Foods on Postprandial Hypotension and Its Relationship to Risk of Cardiovascular Disease and Mortality Outcomes
abstract: The glycemic index (GI) assesses carbohydrate quality by its impact on postprandial glycemia and has been linked to outcomes including postprandial hypotension (PPH) and cardiovascular disease (CVD). We conducted a pilot randomized controlled trial (RCT) to determine PPH prevalence and whether high/low GI meals produce a PPH effect in older adults with type 2 diabetes/hyperlipidemia. No PPH was observed, but the high GI meal showed small but significantly greater drops in diastolic blood pressure (DBP) and rises in pulse compared to the low GI meal. Our pilot RCT highlights the need for larger RCTs on more vulnerable populations investigating the true GI effect on PPH. Additionally, we conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis (SRMA), according to the Cochrane Handbook, which showed that high GI/glycemic load (GL) is associated with higher risk in CVD incidence and mortality outcomes. However, larger cohort studies are needed to provide consistent results.

title: Depression, Emotional Eating, and Cardiovascular Disease Risk in Children and Adolescents
abstract: Depression, a burdensome mental health disorder, increases risk of early cardiovascular disease (CVD) in youth. Although the mechanism of association is unknown, emotional eating, an eating behaviour corresponding to eating in response to emotions, may contribute as it relates to both depression and obesity. However, this is poorly understood within the context of children and adolescents. This dissertation explores the role of emotional eating in the association between depression and CVD risk in children and adolescents. Based on meta-analysis of the current literature, depressive symptoms and emotional eating are associated in youth. Further analysis of an outpatient psychiatry program reveals the depression-CVD association is present early in the course of illness and emotional eating is associated with increased CVD risk among female youth with depression. These findings offer better understanding of emotional eating in the depression-CVD risk association and provide initial support for eating-targeted preventative interventions among youth with depression.

title: Development and Usability Testing of a Support Tool to Identify Models and Frameworks to Inform the Practice of Knowledge Translation
abstract: Theories, models and frameworks (TMFs) are useful when planning, implementing and evaluating healthcare evidence-based interventions and programs, yet it can be challenging to identify an appropriate TMF for an implementation project. This dissertation developed and tested the usability of Find TMF – an online support tool to help knowledge translation (KT) practitioners (who are doing or supporting implementation practice activities) to identify appropriate models and/or frameworks to inform their work. The methods for tool development were guided by models and evidence on implementation science and user-centered design. Phase 1 consisted of a scoping review using the methods outlined by Arksey and O’Malley and the Joanna Briggs Institute. Two reviewers independently screened the literature and abstracted the data. The scoping review identified 596 studies reporting on the use of 159 KT TMFs. Phase 2 consisted of 24 semi-structured interviews with KT researchers and practitioners to explore barriers and facilitators to identifying and selecting TMFs. To develop the tool, Phase 2 interview findings were used to design and tailor the features and functions of the prototype tool (Find TMF) in Phase 3. This process included mapping, in duplicate, the TMFs from Phase 1 by purpose/aim, implementation stage, and target change level. The tool consisted of 3-4 questions about the user’s implementation project, and its algorithm matched key characteristics of the user’s project with characteristics of different TMFs to produce a list of candidate models or frameworks for consideration. Phase 4 consisted of 10 semi-structured interviews with end-users to test the usability of the prototype tool and administer the System Usability Scale (SUS). Overall, participants found the tool to be simple, user-friendly and visually appealing with a useful output of candidate models/frameworks to consider (mean SUS score 84.5; standard deviation 11.4). Tool improvements included providing additional instruction and guidance on what to expect from the tool and how to use the information in the output table. Find TMF is an easy-to-use online tool that may benefit individuals who support KT practice activities by making the vast number of models/frameworks more accessible, and in doing so, contribute to improving the explicit use of relevant TMFs.

title: Investigating Sport Participation for Improving Physical, Psychological, and Social Health in Adults With Spinal Cord Injury or Disease Who Are Aged 50 Years or Older
abstract: Adults living with spinal cord injury or disease (SCI/D) experience various physical, psychological, and social challenges. Sport is a type of physical activity that can provide diverse health benefits for people with SCI/D. The four projects within this thesis aim to investigate the impact of sport participation on the physical, psychological, and social health of individuals with SCI/D, with a latter emphasis on adults living with SCI/D who are aged 50 years or older. Findings from the first study, a thematic synthesis of qualitative studies, and the second study, a scoping review of quantitative studies, demonstrated that sport participation can provide many physical, psychological, and social benefits for those with SCI/D. Importantly, these two studies revealed that most sport research focuses on adults with SCI/D who are younger than 50 years of age while the majority of Canadians with SCI/D are over age 50 years at injury onset. Therefore, the latter studies aimed to address this gap in the literature. The third study explored the experiences and perceptions of adults with SCI/D aged 50 years or older who participate in sport. Findings from semi-structured interviews revealed that sport is a fun and adaptable physical activity that has potential to optimize physical, psychological, and social health in all people with SCI/D, regardless of age. Moreover, participants expressed a shift in priorities from a focus on maximizing performance to an emphasis on optimizing health. The fourth study built on these prior studies and involved the delivery of a peer-facilitated, virtual handcycling sport program for adults aged 50 years or older with SCI/D. Findings from this fourth study revealed that virtual group-based handcycling may provide some health benefits for this population. This program also highlighted the importance of considering secondary health conditions in aging adults with SCI/D and the potential of virtual physical activity programs to address the health challenges of this aging population. The learnings from this thesis can be used to inform the development of future sport programs that are inclusive of people with SCI/D aged 50 years or older.

title: Meeting Them Where They Are: Experiences, Challenges, and Potential Solutions for Cardiac Care Delivery to People Experiencing Homelessness
abstract: Background: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) and homelessness represent two devastating epidemics in Canada. People experiencing homelessness have increased CVD morbidity and mortality. Despite agreement on the urgent need to tailor CVD programs to the homeless population’s competing priorities, effective approaches to cardiac care delivery are under-studied and under-developed in practice. This dissertation aimed to better understand the experiences of people experiencing homelessness with CVD toward identifying potential solutions to improve cardiac care delivery in Toronto using a community-based participatory research (CBPR) approach.Methods: I conducted a systematic scoping review of the international academic and grey literature to characterize the current state of knowledge and practice of CVD management for people experiencing homelessness. To better understand which methods, practices, and values are appropriate for CBPR in the context of CVD in the homeless community, I conducted amethodological case study and co-designed a CBPR approach in partnership with peer researchers with lived experience of homelessness. Based on this CBPR approach, I conducted a multi-method qualitative needs assessment, including demographic surveys, observational fieldwork, interviews, and an adaptive photovoice exercise with people experiencing homelessness with CVD and multi-disciplinary care providers in Toronto, to explore their experiences, perceived needs and barriers, and potential solutions.Findings: Paper 1 reports scoping review findings including challenges and recommendations at the patient encounter, clinic organization, and systems levels. Paper 2 reports CBPR case study findings, which demonstrates the role of and methodological significance of strong community partnerships, advisory relationships, peer researchers, and personal reflections in CBPR. Paper 3 reports findings of the qualitative study and describes research participants’ care experiences, needs and priorities, key enablers and barriers to care of different delivery settings and models, and main recommendations to improve cardiac care delivery for people experiencing homelessness in Toronto.Conclusion: Overall, this work contributes a strong voice of lived experience to the collective research efforts on homeless care delivery. It is relevant to shaping individual clinical practices, as well as situating health research in the larger structures of housing and homelessness. In future work, I hope these practical recommendations can guide the development of a cardiac care program in our local context that meets people where they are.

title: Low Tidal Volume Ventilation in Critically Ill Patients With Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure
abstract: Low tidal volume ventilation (LTVV) leads to lower mortality and fewer secondary organ failures among patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and is associated with shorter durations of invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) and fewer pulmonary complications among patients without ARDS. Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (AHRF) is closely related to ARDS and shares similar diagnostic criteria, etiologies, and clinical outcomes; thus, LTVV may be associated with additional clinical benefits among these patients who do not have ARDS. Unfortunately, LTVV remains underused among patients with and without ARDS. To summarize and compare different interventions designed to improve the delivery of LTVV to critically ill patients undergoing IMV, we performed a systematic review and meta-analyses. We identified 23 studies with 28,971 patients. Several interventions, especially those which combined provider education with audit and feedback and/or protocols, as well as clinical decision support systems in electronic health records were associated with delivery of lower tidal volumes. However, the reductions in tidal volumes rarely achieved the threshold of LTVV and were seldom associated with clinical benefits. To identify patient-level variables that are associated with the delivery of LTVV among patients with AHRF, we conducted a retrospective, observational, multicenter cohort study that included 6,635 patients. We found that height, peak inspiratory pressure, and pH are associated with increased odds of LTVV; weight, sedation-analgesia scale score, and Glasgow coma score are associated with lower odds of LTVV. These results suggest that direct measurement of height and calculation of predicted body weight may improve the delivery of LTVV. Finally, to assess the association between LTVV and clinical outcomes in patients with AHRF, we performed a retrospective, observational cohort study of 8,641 patients. The number of LTVV days were not independently associated with intensive care unit (ICU) or hospital mortality, or with non-pulmonary organ failures. We did identify a non-significant association suggesting that increased adherence to LTVV may be associated with lower ICU mortality, and this warrants further study.

title: Implementation and Effect of Interventions Promoting Social Connections in Older Adults Through Intersectoral Collaborations Involving Primary Care in Catalonia
abstract: Older adults experience reduced social connections due to age-related changes, declining health, and socioeconomic factors, resulting in heightened morbidity and mortality. Collaborative intersectoral strategies involving organizations from health and non-health sectors are necessary to address this multi-faceted problem. Examining a public health program in Catalonia, Spain, this thesis aimed to (a) characterize intersectoral interventions involving primary care to enhance older adults’ social connections, (b) determine their effects on the social support and mental well-being of older residents in Catalonia, and (c) assess their local implementation.Four studies were conducted. Study 1 is a scoping review characterizing intersectoral interventions addressing social connection problems involving primary care. Study 2 introduces a method that combines web scraping, text processing, and spatial analysis (WeTMS) to identify the regional availability of intersectoral asset-based initiatives of this kind across Catalonia. Study 3 is a multilevel cross-sectional analysis examining the association between regional availability of intersectoral asset-based initiatives, social support and mental well-being of older residents in Catalonia. Study 4 is a multi-method implementation evaluation informed by RE-AIM and PRISM frameworks involving surveys, interviews with stakeholders, and document analysis.The scoping review showed a rise in intersectoral interventions involving primary care to foster social connection among older adults since 2014 with heterogeneous characteristics. Through applying the WeTMS method, 9,546 community health assets integrated in intersectoral action, with potential to enhance older adults’ social connections, were identified. The multilevel analysis suggested that residing in regions with more asset-based intersectoral initiatives was positively associated with higher social support and mental well-being compared to regions without such activities. However, the effect sizes were small. The results of the implementation evaluation revealed that the public health program facilitated the successful implementation of nineteen intersectoral interventions in eleven local health areas from 2016 to 2022, reaching 1,754 older adults. Thirteen interventions (68.4%) became permanent services, attributed largely to intersectoral collaborations.Public health programs that promote local intersectoral collaboration can facilitate the implementation of initiatives that foster older adults’ social connections. This thesis provides valuable insights for researchers and policymakers on intersectoral strategies involving primary care to enhance older adults’ social connections.

title: Family Physicians’ Perceived Needs Regarding Their Mental Health and Well-Being in Infectious Catastrophic Events: A Mixed Studies Literature Review
abstract: Introduction:During a catastrophe, the importance of physicians' mental health cannot be overstated. Physicians are exposed to high levels of stress, trauma, and emotional challenges as they navigate through difficult and overwhelming situations. Neglecting their mental health can lead to a wide range of mental issues varied from compassion fatigue to burn out. Therefore, prioritizing the mental health of physicians during a catastrophe is crucial for their own well-being and the well-being of those they serve. This investigation delved into existing evidence about the challenges and perceived needs faced by family physicians in confronting an infectious catastrophe. By addressing the perceived needs of physicians regarding their well-being, healthcare organizations can enhance the effectiveness of mental health interventions and strategies, ultimately improving the well-being and resilience of physicians in the face of a catastrophe.Methodology and methods:A mixed methods systematic review was conducted. The search encompassed two databases, Ovid MEDLINE ALL (1946-February 2023), and PsycINFO on Ovid (1806-February 2023). The Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT) was used to assess the methodological quality. The data extracted from the selected studies were analyzed employing a data-based convergent mixed methods design, enabling the integration of qualitative and quantitative findings.Results:A total of thirty-four studies met the criteria for data extraction. Rigorous line-by-line coding was applied to both Result and Discussion sections of included articles. Findings were systematically categorized into four levels of analysis: Societal, Institutional, Organizational, and Individual. Each level has an overarching theme, except Organizational level encompassing four informative themes. Seven themes emerged, including: “Physician Social Challenges amid Infectious Catastrophes: The Need for Social Support and Inclusion", “Diverse Institutional Challenges in Infectious Catastrophes: From Policy Failures to Systemic Innovations", “Enhancing the Capacity of Family Physicians to Navigate Work Environment Challenges”, “Empowering Family Physicians to Overcome Managerial Issues”, “Assist Family Physicians to Have an Enhanced Team Dynamic”, “Improving Physician Safety by Addressing Protection Challenges”, and “Moral Challenges in Healthcare Decision-Making and Delivery”.Discussion:Public health authorities must focus on establishing organizational frameworks that facilitate coordination within the organization family physicians work in as well as effective collaboration between different organizations. In these institution clinicians must be involved in decision-making processes, clear communication is crucial, alongside mental health support, and adequate staff and resources. At the systemic viewpoint, policy implications underscore the necessity for healthcare policies prioritizing physician well-being, and organizational support to enhance resilience and response effectiveness during infectious catastrophes. Improving physicians' work-related conditions extends beyond PPE access, requiring swift betterment of service innovations like telemedicine, with ongoing reassessment for sustainability in care planning, financing, and delivery beyond the emergency phase.Alternate abstract:Introduction :Pendant une catastrophe, l'importance de la santé mentale des médecins ne peut être surestimée. Les médecins sont exposés à des niveaux élevés de stress, de traumatisme et de défis émotionnels alors qu'ils naviguent dans des situations difficiles et accablantes. Négliger leur santé mentale peut entraîner une large gamme de problèmes mentaux allant de la fatigue de la compassion à l'épuisement professionnel. Par conséquent, prioriser la santé mentale des médecins pendant une catastrophe est crucial pour leur propre bien-être et celui de ceux qu'ils servent. Cette revue de la littérature a exploré les défis et les besoins perçus auxquels font face les médecins de famille lors de la confrontation à une catastrophe infectieuse. En répondant aux besoins perçus des médecins en ce qui concerne leur bien-être, les organisations de santé peuvent améliorer l'efficacité des interventions et des stratégies en santé mentale, ce qui finalement pourrait renforcer le bien-être et la résilience des médecins face à une catastrophe.Méthodologie et méthodes :Une revue systématique à méthodes mixtes a été menée. La recherche a couvert deux bases de données de renom, à savoir Ovid MEDLINE ALL (1946-février 2023) et PsycINFO sur Ovid (1806-février 2023). L'outil d'évaluation des méthodes mixtes (MMAT) a été utilisé pour évaluer la qualité méthodologique. Les données extraites des études sélectionnées ont été analysées en utilisant une conception de méthodes mixtes convergentes basée sur les données, permettant l'intégration rigoureuse des résultats qualitatifs et quantitatifs.Résultats :Trente-quatre études ont répondu aux critères d'extraction des données. Un codage rigoureux, ligne par ligne, a été appliqué aux sections Résultats et Discussion des articles inclus. Les conclusions ont été systématiquement catégorisées en quatre niveaux d'analyse : Sociétal, Institutionnel, Organisationnel et Individuel. Chaque niveau inclut un thème principal, à l'exception du niveau Organisationnel qui en englobe quatre. Sept thèmes ont alors émergé, nommés tel qui suit : (1) "Défis sociaux des médecins face aux catastrophes infectieuses : le besoin de soutien social et d'inclusion", (2) "Divers défis institutionnels dans les catastrophes infectieuses : des échecs de politique aux innovations systémiques", (3) "Renforcer la capacité des médecins de famille à faire face aux défis de l'environnement de travail", (4) "Autonomiser les médecins de famille pour surmonter les problèmes de gestion", (5) "Aider les médecins de famille à avoir une dynamique d'équipe améliorée", (6) "Améliorer la sécurité des médecins en abordant les défis de protection", et (7) "Défis moraux dans la prise de décision et la prestation de soins de santé".Discussion :Les autorités de santé publique devraient travailler avec les médecins de famille dans l'établissement de cadres organisationnels facilitant la coordination, et ce, au sein des organisations et entre elles. Être activement impliqué dans les processus de prise de décision et une communication claire sont cruciales dans ce processus, ainsi que le soutien en santé mentale et des ressources adéquates et du personnel au sein des organisations de soins de santé. Dans une perspective plus large, cette revue souligne la nécessité de formuler des politiques de santé priorisant le bien-être des médecins, et un soutien organisationnel pour renforcer la résilience et l'efficacité de la réponse lors des catastrophes infectieuses.

title: Understanding and Optimizing the Sustainability and Sustainment of Newly Implemented Evidence-Based Practices in Rehabilitation
abstract: To optimize quality of care, evidence-based practices are implemented into rehabilitation practice. A growing body of research suggests that newly implemented evidence-based practices are only sustained post-implementation 40-60% of the time. Poor sustainment can result in a loss of potential improvements in quality of care and patient outcomes.Although descriptions of the contextual factors that affect sustainability and sustainability strategies exist, there is a paucity of evidence on how (i.e., mechanisms by which) certain sustainability strategies and contextual features produce sustainability outcomes. The lack of research linking sustainability strategies, context and outcomes means that there is little guidance on how to optimize sustainment. The overall aim of this thesis was to understand and optimize the sustainability and sustainment of a newly implemented evidence-based practice in three rehabilitation centers.To operationalize the overarching aim, this thesis is composed of four projects. All projects were informed by an integrated knowledge translation approach whereby researchers and diverse clinical stakeholders actively collaborated to optimize implementation and research success.Project 1: To understand how (mechanisms) and in what circumstances (context) a rehabilitation practice is sustained, or not (outcome) I conducted a realist review. The program theory generated in this review proposes three patterns: (1) implementation and sustainability phases are interconnected, (2) the continued use of the evidence-based practice can be interpreted as the critical sustainability outcome, and (3) intermediate sustainability outcomes (i.e., fit/alignment, financial support, benefits, expertise) can become contextual features influencing other sustainability outcomes.Project 2: To describe the collaborative sustainability planning process for an outcome measure I conducted a qualitative description study. Planning was guided by the program theory. Three themes captured the collaborative sustainability planning process: (1) collaboration as a driver for sustainability (2) co-creation of a sustainability plan to achieve shared objectives and (3) the iterative nature of sustainability planning.Project 3: To understand how (mechanisms) and in what circumstances (context) a rehabilitation outcome measure is sustained, or not (outcome) I conducted a realist evaluation using a mixed method, embedded single case study design. The program theory developed in project 1 was tested and refined, resulting in four patterns: (1) implementation and sustainability phases are interconnected, (2) outcomes build on each other cyclically with patient benefits as the keystone outcome (3) sustainment is achieved to varying levels across different sustainability outcomes and (4) the work of sustaining the outcome measure is divided amongst stakeholders.Project 4: To evaluate clinician’s reaction, learning and behavioural intent to a collaboratively developed training session for an outcome measure I used an explanatory sequential mixed method design. Survey participants rated themselves higher on most learning outcomes (knowledge, confidence, and skill) and scoring behavioural intent between 1-week pre-training, and 1- and 8-weeks post-training. Interview participants explained these survey results: 1) supporting positive reactions to the training session, and its learning and behavioural impacts, 2) indicating that their negative attitudes and commitment are due to limitations in the outcome measure, and, 3) explaining that impacts were affected by contextual factors.This thesis advances our understanding of sustainability and sustainment, especially of outcome measures. Researchers can continue to test and refine the program theories while implementation teams can apply thesis findings to optimize their success, ultimately contributing to sustained high quality care and optimized patient outcomesAlternate abstract:Pour optimiser la qualité des soins, des pratiques fondées sur des données probantes sont mises en œuvre dans la pratique de la réadaptation. De plus en plus d'études suggèrent que les pratiques fondées sur des données probantes nouvellement mises en œuvre ne sont pérennisées que dans 40-60% des cas. Une pérennisation insuffisante peut entraîner une perte des améliorations potentielles de la qualité des soins et des résultats pour les patients.Bien qu'il existe des descriptions des facteurs contextuels qui affectent la pérennité et les stratégies de pérennité, il y a peu de preuves sur la façon (c'est-à-dire les mécanismes par lesquels) certaines stratégies de pérennité et caractéristiques contextuelles produisent des résultats de pérennité. Le manque de recherche reliant les stratégies de pérennité, le contexte et les résultats signifie qu'il y a peu de conseils sur la façon d'optimiser la pérennité. L'objectif global de cette thèse est de comprendre et d'optimiser la pérennité et la pérennisation d'une pratique fondée sur des données probantes récemment mise en œuvre dans trois centres de réadaptation.Pour concrétiser l'objectif général, cette thèse est composée de quatre projets. Tous les projets ont été guidés par une approche intégrée de l'application des connaissances dans laquelle les chercheurs et les diverses parties prenantes cliniques ont activement collaboré pour optimiser la mise en œuvre et le succès de la recherche.Projet 1: Pour comprendre comment (mécanismes) et dans quelles circonstances (contexte) une pratique de réadaptation est pérennisée ou non (résultat), j'ai mené une revue réaliste. La théorie du programme générée par cet examen propose trois tendances : (1) les phases de mise en œuvre et de pérennité sont interconnectées, (2) l'utilisation continue de la pratique fondée sur des données probantes peut être interprétée comme le résultat critique de la pérennité, et (3) les résultats intermédiaires de la pérennité (c'est-àdire l'adéquation/l'alignement, le soutien financier, les avantages, l'expertise) peuvent devenir des caractéristiques contextuelles qui influencent d'autres résultats de la pérennité.Projet 2 : Pour décrire le processus de planification collaborative de la pérennité en vue d'une mesure des résultats, j'ai mené une étude descriptive qualitative. La xiii planification a été guidée par la théorie du programme. Trois thèmes ont permis d'appréhender le processus de planification collaborative de la pérennité : (1) la collaboration en tant que moteur de la pérennité (2) la co-création d'un plan de pérennité pour atteindre des objectifs communs et (3) la nature itérative de la planification de la pérennité.Projet 3 : Pour comprendre comment (mécanismes) et dans quelles circonstances (contexte) une mesure des résultats de la réadaptation est pérennisée ou non (résultat), j'ai mené une évaluation réaliste utilisant une seule étude de cas à méthode mixte. La théorie du programme développée dans le projet 1 a été testée et affinée, ce qui a permis de dégager quatre modèles : (1) les phases de mise en œuvre et de pérennité sont interconnectées, (2) les résultats se construisent les uns sur les autres de manière cyclique, les bénéfices pour les patients étant le résultat clé, (3) la pérennisation est atteinte à des niveaux variables à travers différents résultats de pérennité et (4) le travail de pérennité du MPAI-4 est partagé entre les parties prenantes.Projet 4 : Pour évaluer la réaction, les apprentissages et les intentions comportementales des cliniciens à l'égard d'une session de formation élaborée en collaboration pour une mesure des résultats, j'ai utilisé une méthode mixte séquentielle explicative. Les participants au sondage se sont mieux évalués pour la plupart des résultats d'apprentissage (connaissances, confiance et compétences) et ont noté leur intention comportementale entre une semaine avant la formation et une semaine et huit semaines après la formation. Les participants aux entretiens ont expliqué ces résultats du sondage: 1) en soutenant les réactions positives à la session de formation et ses impacts sur l'apprentissage et le comportement, 2) en indiquant que leurs attitudes négatives et leur engagement sont dus aux limites de la mesure des résultats, et 3) en expliquant que les impacts ont été affectés par des facteurs contextuels

title: Investigating Impacts of Twenty-Four-Hour Movement Behaviours on Healthy Ageing in Older Adults
abstract: The impacts of 24-hour movement behaviours [a term that collectively refers to physical activity (PA), sedentary behaviour (SB), and sleep] on healthy ageing in older adults is understudied. Of this limited literature, the extent of associations between movement behaviours and healthy ageing outcomes are largely hampered by flawed methodological approaches for analysing movement behaviour data. My aim with this thesis research is to use an integrated approach to more closely examine associations between daily movement behaviours and two key determinants of healthy ageing: quality of life (QOL) and depression symptoms. The objectives are to: 1) synthesise and critically examine the literature on movement behaviours and QOL; 2) employ compositional data analysis to examine associations between movement behaviours and QOL and depression symptoms, respectively to determine if associations still hold, and how effects of changing time spent between movement behaviours impact these two outcomes relative to the time spent in remaining behaviours, focussing exclusively on the Canadian older adult population ≥ 65 years of age.Manuscript one is the first to systematically review the literature on movement behaviours and QOL in older adults using an integrated movement behaviour approach. It is also the first to represent QOL by distinct domains, and to categorise and group study results accordingly. Overall, the findings indicate that movement behaviours are associated with QOL. Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) is favourably associated with QOL. The evidence regarding light-intensity physical activity (LIPA), SB and sleep duration is inconsistent.Subsequently, two empirical studies applied a compositional data analysis framework to data from the Canadian Longitudinal Study on Aging.  Results from both analyses show that older Canadians spend most of their time across the day in SB and sleep, with limited activity and that daily movement behaviour composition is strongly associated with QOL and depression symptoms, respectively. In manuscript two, results show how reduction in time spent sedentary and increases in PA and sleep improve QOL. In manuscript three, results show how replacing time in MVPA with equivalent time from any other behaviour is associated with increase in depression symptoms. In both manuscripts, stratified analyses indicate that effects differ by sex and age group (younger old versus older old).Collectively, the findings from this thesis research demonstrate that the way older adults spend their time in movement behaviours throughout the course of a day has implications for healthy ageing. The work provides new avenues for researchers to explore how 24-hour movement behaviours are related to older adult health.

title: It takes a village: Exploring the effect of antibiotics and other modulators on raising the infant gut microbiota
abstract: The research described in this thesis investigated the impact of antibiotic exposure during early infancy on the development of the intestinal microbiota and its consequences on later health. It covers microbial transmission, a literature review on neonatal antibiotic exposure and health outcomes, and a specific exploration of infant colic and its association with microbiota development. The findings emphasize the need for careful consideration of antibiotic exposure, stewardship programs, and further research to address the long-term consequences of microbiota (transmission) perturbations in early life. In Chapter 1 the foundation is laid for understanding the human microbiota's complexity throughout the human body, with a focus on the intestinal microbiota's origin, composition, and function. The development of a new microbial ecosystem in the gastrointestinal tract of infants depends on sources of microbes that can originate from the mother through vertical transmission, see Chapter 2, or horizontal transfer. Horizontal transmission is determined by environmental exposure through food and water, social contact, and exposure through globalization. This introductory chapter establishes the need to explore the impact of antibiotic exposure during a critical phase in early microbiota development. The Dutch INCA study, which is the infant cohort used throughout the thesis, is introduced. Chapter 2 delves into the vertical transmission of microbes from mother to child. The review discusses three determinants of transmission—maternal microbiota sources, maternal contact, and engraftment in the infant recipient. It concludes that factors such as the mother's genotype, diet, health status, and antimicrobial exposure shape maternal microbiota sources. Delivery mode and feeding choices influence maternal contact, while host habitat filtering, established microbes, and antibiotic disruptions determine engraftment in the infant. The chapter calls for future research to address the gaps in understanding maternal transmissions essential for fostering a healthy infant microbiota.

title: Early Detection of Polyneuropathy in Patients With Hereditary Transthyretin Amyloid Cardiomyopathy and Determining the Cost-effective Treatment for Mixed Phenotype Patients
abstract: Transthyretin amyloidosis is a rare disease caused by the deposits of misfolded proteins known as amyloid. This disease is caused by misfolded transthyretin (TTR) proteins, that are produced in the liver and are deposited in various organs and tissues, especially in the heart and nerves. These TTR proteins are normally formed as stable tetramers; however, a mutation on the TTR gene could cause the dissociation of the TTR tetramer into monomers which would then form as TTR amyloid causing transthyretin amyloidosis (ATTR). Patients with ATTR may present cardiomyopathy and/or polyneuropathy. Although there is no cure for this disease, there are treatments available that would slow down the progression of ATTR and allow patients to have a better quality of life (QOL). The treatments include organ transplantation (liver/heart) or novel disease modifying therapies.This thesis focuses on detecting polyneuropathy among patients with hereditary transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy and determining its cost-effective treatment.

title: The value of standards for health datasets in artificial intelligence-based applications
abstract: Artificial intelligence as a medical device is increasingly being applied to healthcare for diagnosis, risk stratification and resource allocation. However, a growing body of evidence has highlighted the risk of algorithmic bias, which may perpetuate existing health inequity. This problem arises in part because of systemic inequalities in dataset curation, unequal opportunity to participate in research and inequalities of access. This study aims to explore existing standards, frameworks and best practices for ensuring adequate data diversity in health datasets. Exploring the body of existing literature and expert views is an important step towards the development of consensus-based guidelines. The study comprises two parts: a systematic review of existing standards, frameworks and best practices for healthcare datasets; and a survey and thematic analysis of stakeholder views of bias, health equity and best practices for artificial intelligence as a medical device. We found that the need for dataset diversity was well described in literature, and experts generally favored the development of a robust set of guidelines, but there were mixed views about how these could be implemented practically. The outputs of this study will be used to inform the development of standards for transparency of data diversity in health datasets (the STANDING Together initiative).A systematic review, combined with a stakeholder survey, presents an overview of current practices and recommendations for dataset curation in health, with specific focuses on data diversity and artificial intelligence-based applications.

title: Factors affecting access to dialysis for patients with end-stage kidney disease in Sub-Saharan Africa: A scoping review
abstract: AimsThis scoping review examined the factors affecting access to dialysis for patients with end-stage kidney disease in Sub-Saharan Africa.DesignScoping review.MethodsThe scoping review is conducted following the Joanna Briggs Institute methodology for scoping reviews and modelled by Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-analyses extension for scoping review.ResultsA descriptive content analysis of 30 included articles revealed three main findings affecting access and use of dialysis: Health system–related factors, health provider–related factors and patient factors.Patient or Public ContributionEquity in renal replacement therapy access and use will require concerted advocacy for good public policy, healthcare delivery, workforce capacity and education.

title: The Role of Novel Hepatic Assessment Tools in the Treatment of Colorectal Liver Metastases: The CLiFF and CoNoR Studies
abstract: Introduction (chapter 1): This thesis comprises two projects - the CLiFF and CoNoR studies; both using different methodologies to investigate the role of novel assessment tools in optimising the management of colorectal liver metastases (CLM). CLiFF Methods (chapter 2): The CLiFF study prospectively measured chemotherapy-associated changes in liver function (hepatocyte injury) and liver fat (hepatic steatosis) prior to liver resection for CLM, using the novel techniques of the LiMAx (Liver Maximum Capacity) test and advanced magnetic resonance imaging (spectroscopy, modified Dixon method, and LiverMultiScan). These changes were corelated with standard clinical assessment results, anthropometric measures (BMI, SAT, VAT and WC), histological assessment of steatosis and post-operative complications. The study was powered to require 24 patients and was on track to achieve the recruitment target of 35 participants, prior to premature termination at 26 patients due to COVID. CoNoR Methods (chapter 3): The CoNoR study tested the hypothesis that novel tests (LiMAx test and MR Hepatica scan) could potentially reduce variation in CLM resectability decision-making via four workstreams: (WS1) systematic review of CLM technical resectability criteria; (WS2) International HPB MDT interviews; (WS3) International HPB MDT questionnaires to establish current practice and to inform the final design of the (WS4) international case-based online survey, finally assessing the added value of the added novel tests. CLiFF Results (chapter 4): In CLiFF, liver fat increased and liver function decreased after chemotherapy as expected, but neither change was clinically significant. A sub-group of patients had significant increases in hepatic fat, which might be clinically relevant. A new hypothesis was generated, that both oxaliplatin- and irinotecan-based systemic chemotherapies might be associated with hepatic injury but that the recovery from irinotecan might be faster than that from oxaliplatin. The patterns of changes in LiMAx values after chemotherapy were similar to previously published data, and this was the first reported quantified change in liver fat after chemotherapy. There was no correlation between change in liver fat and liver function. CoNoR Results (chapter 5): For CoNoR, the systematic review showed that there is ongoing variation in the technical resectability criteria reported in the literature. WS3 (197 participants from six continents) revealed that many HPB experts seek additional information in pre-operative assessment, beyond the currently available tests, and that many were open to assessing whether either Hepatica or LiMAx might add additional helpful information. Conclusions: CLiFF demonstrated, using novel minimally-invasive assessment tools, that pre-operative systemic chemotherapy is associated with increased hepatic fat accumulation and hepatic tissue injury. These changes were not statistically significant — this might be due to small numbers due to the pre-mature termination of this study due to COVID. These data can now be used to power a larger-scale study.

title: Geographical Disparities in Access to Systemic Anti Cancer Therapies for Advanced Breast Cancer Across Greater Manchester
abstract: Introduction:There were 48,387 new cases of breast cancer in England in 2019 with almost 10,000 related deaths. Up to 99% of these deaths were due to secondary (metastatic) breast cancer. Secondary breast cancer has been defined as the development of tumors in tissues and organs away from the primary tumour site. Although treatable with guideline recommended systemic anti-cancer therapies, secondary breast cancer remains incurable. Geographical disparities exist however there is limited knowledge as to how these impact treatment access for women with secondary breast cancer. The aim of the thesis was to evaluate geographical disparities in access to systemic anti-cancer therapies for women with secondary breast cancer across the Greater Manchester region.Method:A systematic review of the literature informed the development of a mixed methods study. A retrospective cohort study using routinely collected clinical data investigated geographic variables and receipt of first line guideline concordant systemic anti-cancer therapies (received within 31 days of secondary breast cancer diagnosis) in a multivariate regression analysis. To explore patient and clinician experiences of access to systemic anti-cancer therapies a pragmatic qualitative study using semi structured interviews was undertaken. Findings from each were integrated in a multi paradigmatic discussion.Results:The systematic review included 15 non UK based studies of a quantitative design; no qualitative studies met the inclusion criteria. Disparities in treatment access were found to be associated with different geographical regions, health care facilities and location of treatment center. The retrospective cohort study identified geographical disparities associated with receipt of guideline concordant systemic anti-cancer therapies. Women who received treatment at centres located across the Greater Manchester region had lower odds of treatment receipt compared with those treated at the specialist cancer center (OR 0.10 95% CI: 0.01 to 0.79).) In the qualitative study both women and clinicians described a preference for proximity to the treatment center as this conferred a perceived level of safety and security. Clinicians stated that the availability of different systemic anti-cancer therapies was variable across treatment sites. All interview participants commented that the Covid-19 pandemic presented challenges related to systemic anti-cancer treatment.Discussion:This mixed methods study provides a novel contribution to the evidence relating to access to systemic anti-cancer therapies for the treatment of secondary breast cancer in the UK. Findings indicated a need for a health equity impact assessment and greater health care system equity in treatment access. The study provided evidence which highlighted the need for improved availability of high quality routinely collected data for SBC to inform treatment access. Generalisability was however limited due to restriction in sample size as a result availability of routinely collected data for this patient population.Implications for practice, policy and research:Findings identified the need for targeted interventions to promote greater equity in treatment access. This should be addressed through commission strategies via the Greater Manchester Integrated Care Partnership in conjunction with Greater Manchester Cancer. Improvements in the availability and quality of routinely collected data are required.

title: Masculinities, Social Support, Relationships, and Community: A Qualitative Exploration of How Social Factors Contribute to Psychological Distress and Suicidality in Men
abstract: The aim of this thesis was to qualitatively explore the role of certain social factors in contributing to psychological distress and suicidality in men. It comprises three papers: A meta-synthesis; an empirical qualitative study; and a critical reflective review of both research papers. Paper 1 has been prepared for submission to Psychology of Men & Masculinities. The paper presents a systematic qualitative meta-synthesis of studies reporting on the role of masculinities in relation to suicidality, from the perspectives of men who had experienced suicidality. Twenty-five studies were included. Thematic synthesis was used to analyse the findings. Three themes were developed which focussed on men's views of how masculinities may contribute to, and/or protect against, suicidality and help-seeking for suicidality: 1) Strength, Stoicism, Suicide and Help-Seeking (consisting of two subthemes); 2) Suicide as a Masculine vs a Non-Masculine Act; 3) Power and Suicide (consisting of three subthemes). The clinical implications of these findings and recommendations for future research are discussed. Paper 2 has been prepared for submission to the Journal of Gay & Lesbian Mental Health. It is a qualitative study that sought to explore the role of social support, relationships, masculinities and community in contributing to psychological distress in young gay men. Eight men participated in the study. Verbatim semi-structured interview transcriptions were analysed using Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA). Four themes were developed which focussed on men's views of how their social experiences have impacted their distress, wellbeing, identities and relationships: 1) Growing up Gay in a Straight World: Developmental Traumas (consisting of four subthemes); 2) Gender Policing and Performance: Masculinity and Misogyny; 3) Belonging and Not Belonging within LGBTQ+ Communities (consisting of two subthemes); and 4) Relational Responses to Rejection (consisting of four subthemes). The clinical implications for individual and systemic psychological interventions with gay men are discussed, as are recommendations for future research. Finally, Paper 3 consists of a critical appraisal of conducting the studies in Papers 1 and 2. This includes a discussion of methodological decisions made, the strengths and limitations of how the studies were conducted, and the relevance of the studies' findings to clinical practice and existing theories and research. The paper also offers the author's reflections on completing the studies and on the implications of the findings for her personal and professional life.

title: Prevention of Second-Hand Smoke Exposure Among Pregnant Women and Children in Egypt and the Rest of the Middle East: A Mixed-Methods Investigation
abstract: Second-hand smoke (SHS) exposure is a major public health concern. Pregnant women and children are a priority population for tobacco control efforts because second-hand smoke (SHS) exposure during pregnancy/childhood poses serious risks to foetal/child health. Due to strong cultural constraints against women smoking in many Middle Eastern countries, the prevalence of tobacco smoking is higher among men than women, which puts non-smoking women and children on risk of exposure to SHS. The Middle Eastern countries are Bahrain, Cyprus, Egypt, Iran, Iraq, Israel, Jordan, Kuwait, Lebanon, Oman, Palestine, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Syria, Turkey, United Arab Emirates, and Yemen.In Egypt, daily SHS exposure among pregnant non-smoking women and children is estimated to be more than 50% at home and more than 70% in public places. The aim of the current thesis is to investigate the experience of SHS exposure among pregnant women and children in Middle Eastern countries with focus on Egypt, barriers and facilitators to reduce it, and to come up with recommendations on how to reduce this exposure. Three studies were conducted to achieve this aim.The first study aimed to identify, appraise, and synthesize the evidence related to experiences and views of parents, children, and professionals on the prevention of second-hand smoke exposure to women and children in Middle Eastern countries by conducting a qualitative systematic review. Six databases and grey literature were searched from inception to January 2021 to identify published and unpublished studies. No language restrictions were applied. The JBI guidelines for qualitative systematic reviews were followed in conducting the review, with a meta-aggregation process was used to synthesize findings and ConQUAL used to summarise the confidence in the findings. Of 5229 records identified, two qualitative studies (in three publications) met the eligibility criteria and were included in the review. One study was conducted in Turkey and the other study (reported in two papers) was conducted in Israel. The methodological quality of the studies was high based on the JBI critical appraisal tool for qualitative research. The participants in the included studies were parents (n=118 participants) aged between 18 and 42 years. One of the included studies did not differentiate in quotations between mothers and fathers. The methods used for data collection within the included studies were interviews, which were analysed using thematic analysis.A total of 50 findings were extracted and aggregated into eight categories, based on the similarity of meaning. Three synthesized finding were generated, all with moderate confidence: i) Parents were aware of SHS exposure and that exposure is harmful, although the health dangers of SHS exposure were not commonly discussed with parents by Healthcare Professionals (HCPs) during pregnancy; ii) Smoking is a socially and culturally accepted norm, with parents reporting cultural beliefs about traditional values as a barrier to reducing SHS exposure in the home. Parents also reported personal psychological barriers to quitting smoking; iii) Parents implemented different physical restrictions on smoking, such as having rules about where smoking can take place at home, but tended to lack certainty or confidence regarding whether such protective measures were needed or would be effective. As this systematic review found that SHS exposure is not commonly discussed with parents during pregnancy by HCPs, it recommends standardized guidelines to be available for HCPs in primary health centres to help them to guide parents regarding SHS exposure during pregnancy and childhood.

title: Yoga for Hypertension Management in the UK – What is the Evidence?
abstract: Yoga is an ancient Indian philosophy and way of life that is being used as a means to improve health and well-being. Numerous studies have shown that yoga has positive effects on managing many non-communicable diseases. Moreover, growing evidence suggests that yoga could be used to improve the management of hypertension in addition to current management strategies. Yoga has also become increasingly popular in the world and the UK for improving health and well-being, and previous cross-sectional studies have reported that hypertension is one of the most commonly disclosed health conditions in yoga sessions in the UK. The popularity of yoga has surged globally, including in the UK. However, the term "yoga" encompasses a broad range of concepts and previous systematic reviews included studies from across the world including places where yoga could mean different things. Despite the increasing body of evidence supporting yoga's benefits for hypertension and overall well-being, research specifically focusing on yoga in the UK remains limited. This thesis aimed to bridge this gap by exploring the current landscape of yoga practice in the UK and its potential for managing hypertension. Three studies were conducted for this purpose, aiming to gain a comprehensive understanding of the provision of yoga in the country.The aim of the first study was to synthesise the content, structure and delivery characteristics of effective yoga interventions used for managing hypertension, and to compare these characteristics with interventions that were not found to be effective. Randomised controlled trials (RCTs) conducted among people with hypertension reporting at least one of the major components of yoga (i.e., asana, pranayama and dhyana and relaxation practices) and comparing them with no intervention, sham intervention, any non-pharmaceutical intervention (such as diet, exercise, or yoga) or pharmaceutical intervention (such as antihypertensive drugs) were eligible. Sixteen databases were searched for published and unpublished studies without any date and language restrictions till March 15, 2021. The literature search yielded 13,130 records. Thirty-four RCTs (evaluating 38 yoga interventions) met the inclusion criteria. Overall, the included studies had low quality mostly due to inadequate reporting of the methodology. Yoga reduced systolic blood pressure (SBP) and diastolic blood pressure (DBP) compared to a control intervention (mean difference (MD −6.49 and −2.78; 95% confidence interval (CI) −8.94 to −4.04 and −4.11 to −1.45, respectively). Eighteen, 14 and 20 interventions were effective in improving SBP, DBP, or either, respectively. 13 out of 20 effective interventions incorporated all the 3 major components of yoga (i.e., asana (yogic poses), pranayama (breathing practices) and dhyana (meditation) and relaxation practices) and allocated similar durations to each component whereas interventions that were not found to be effective were more focused on the asana and duration of asana practice was longer. The most common duration and frequency of effective interventions were 45 min/session (in 5 interventions), 7 days/week (in 5 interventions), and 12 weeks (in 11 interventions) whereas the most common session frequency was 2 days a week (in 7 interventions) in ineffective interventions. Effective interventions were mostly centre-based (in 15 interventions) and supervised (in 16 interventions), and this was similar to interventions that were not found to be effective.The second study aimed to explore who is providing yoga; what sessions are available, where and at what cost; who attends these sessions in the UK, and whether yoga providers were aware of health conditions in their sessions. A cross-sectional survey was undertaken among yoga providers in the UK. They were approached through four major UK yoga associations. 407 yoga providers participated. Most providers were aged 45-64 years (69%), female (93%), and white (93%). The median number of group sessions and one-to-one sessions delivered per week was four and two, respectively. The most common styles were Hatha (28%), Iyengar (26%), and Vinyasa (15%). Sessions had a varying emphasis on different yogic practices, but 59% of providers allocated most time to yogic poses (asana), 18% to breathing practices (pranayama), and 12% to meditation (dhyana) and relaxation practices. Most (73%) reported that their attendees disclosed their health conditions to them most commonly; mental health issues (41%), hypertension (25%), and heart diseases (9%).The third study aimed to explore yoga providers’ knowledge, experiences, and attitudes toward delivering yoga to people with hypertension. Semi-structured qualitative interviews were conducted with yoga providers in the UK (n=19). Interviews were audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, and analysed thematically. Eight themes were identified. Yoga providers were generally aware of the health conditions of their attendees, and they had a reasonable knowledge of the causes, signs and symptoms, and management of hypertension. Whilst most had received some information about hypertension as part of their initial yoga teaching training, this was generally felt to be limited. They mentioned the biopsychosocial benefits of yoga on hypertension but also expressed their concerns about the lack of regulation, the wide disparity in what is being delivered under the label of yoga, and the competency of some yoga providers.In conclusion, despite some mismatches between yoga sessions in the UK and effective yoga interventions, yoga holds promise for hypertension management in the UK as it was an available and accessible practice in the UK, practised by people with hypertension and yoga providers showed willingness to deliver yoga to people with hypertension. Though the current evidence lacks strength in terms of both quality and quantity, it does indicate some consistency in the characteristics of effective yoga interventions. An important implication from this thesis is that the characteristics of potentially effective yoga interventions were in line with the lifestyle recommendations in the hypertension guideline by the ISH. In addition, considering that yoga is an affordable practice and yoga providers were willing to deliver yoga to people with hypertension, yoga could be of some help with managing hypertension in the UK. However, to establish stronger evidence before recommending the integration of yoga into the NHS for managing hypertension, comprehensive investigations involving different study populations, with a focus on validating and improving the identified intervention characteristics, are needed. Therefore, to carry the work done in this review to the next stage, with inspiration from the findings of this systematic review and meta-analysis, a funding application has been submitted to NIHR to develop a yoga intervention that is likely to lead to maximum benefits for people with high blood pressure and to explore the acceptability and accessibility to people with different physical abilities in the UK.

title: The Optimisation and Evaluation of Public Health Literacy
abstract: Introduction:Health Literacy (HL) is a key skill for the prevention of adverse health and the maintenance and promotion of good health outcomes, and is generally agreed to reflect a person’s ability to understand, access, appraise and apply health information. Due to HL reflecting a person’s skill to navigate health information, public HL has become a high priority in recent times. This growth has, however, expanded in parallel with disputes – and varied interpretations - regarding its conceptualisation. This heterogeneity may therefore lead to reliability, validity, and comparability issues. Moreover, many HL models are formed in reference to adults, and do not include differences in children compared with adults. Children actively engage in health decisions, and understanding potential HL differences is thus essential for supporting the transition towards health autonomy. Clarity on child and adult HL measurement appears unmet currently, and minimal child HL assessments have been conducted across England.Aims:Two aims were outlined: to investigate the conceptual nature of HL in community adult and child populations; and to gather preliminary data on the state of child HL in England.Methods:A scoping review of community HL intervention outcome practices was conducted (Sawyers et al., 2022). An international Delphi study followed this to achieve consensus on best practices for HL measurement in community adult and child populations, with the outcome being the development of a HL measurement framework. Lastly, a pilot study was implemented to assess the feasibility of measuring HL in 10-13 year old children as guided by the framework.Results:Three series of results are presented. Firstly, the review identified a unidimensional profile for direct HL measurement, contrasting previous inferences of a convergence towards holistic practices. When proxy HL was analysed, substantial variation was reported, suggesting deviation in HL measurement beyond the direct domain. The Delphi study used four contexts as the base for the framework: adult direct (n = 12), adult proxy (n = 12), child direct (n = 15) and child proxy (n = 10). Within each context HL outcomes were identified, forming the final framework. The third study recruited a small pilot sample (n = 17), and provided an initial understanding of child HL measurement. Baseline figures for functional HL, communicative HL, critical HL, self-efficacy, health behaviours, and qualitative data for health learning were retrieved, and recruitment challenges through schools were identified as key barriers to a follow-up. Early, frequent communication, flexible research protocols, and school engagement at the project conceptualisation were highlighted as important for successful school recruitment.Discussion:Current literature indications yield HL as multidimensional, but HL is frequently implemented in a unidimensional manner at the direct level and a variable way at the proxy level. Using expert consensus, a framework has been produced to clarify the HL outcomes important to consider in community adult and child populations. The framework aims to foster consistent, comparable, and valid community HL measurement practices, while encouraging researchers and policy stakeholders to consider child and adult HL research with a renewed focus. Though an initial pilot indicates feasibility of the framework, further follow-up is required to better understand child HL in England. Nevertheless, these findings support researchers in measuring adult and child HL more consistently, and provides suggestions when collaborating with primary and secondary schools in England on the basis of pilot study evidence.

title: Optimising Contextual Factors in the Practitioner-Patient Encounter in the Management of Osteoarthritis
abstract: BackgroundContextual factors (CFs) related to the patient, healthcare practitioners, and their therapeutic relationship are integral to the overall treatment effect of any given intervention. In osteoarthritis (OA), around 75% of the treatment effect is directly attributable to CFs. Identifying and understanding the role of CFs may encourage healthcare practitioners to develop and enhance the contextual aspects of care, and thus enhance the overall treatment benefit.ObjectivesThe overall aim of this research project is to develop a contextual enhancement package (CEP) that can be used to optimise the management of OA.
